title: Quantum Magnetometry with Orientation beyond Steady-State Limits in Cavity-Magnon Systems
authors: Zheng Liu, Ding-hui Xu, Yi-jia Yang, Chang-shui Yu
abstract: We present a transient quantum sensing framework for cavity-magnon systems that circumvents the inevitable loss of initial-state quantum properties plaguing conventional steady-state protocols. Explicitly incorporating finite-time dynamics and adopting an engineered steady state as the initial condition, we derive the exact transient noise spectrum. We show that residual initial quantum correlations alone can drastically enhance the short-time signal-to-noise ratio (SNR) beyond that achievable with unsqueezed steady-state schemes. Through analysis of the transient spectral density and joint measurements of orthogonal cavity quadratures, we realize crosstalk-free reconstruction of all three magnetic field components, enabling orientation of magnetic signals. In the long-time limit, our theory yields a closed-form stationary noise spectrum and uncovers a resonance condition $g_{am}=\sqrt{κ_aκ_m}/2$, where cavity field quantum noise is fully canceled without requiring strong coherent coupling. Away from this resonance, injected squeezing further suppresses cavity induced noise and broadens the detection bandwidth. Extending the framework to an array of $N$ yttrium iron garnet (YIG) spheres generates a collective bright mode, with magnon-probe noise scaling as $1/N$. Our results establish a unified route to scalable, high precision, multidimensional quantum magnetometry using cavity-magnon platforms.
summary: This paper proposes a transient quantum sensing method for cavity-magnon systems that outperforms steady-state protocols. By utilizing initial quantum correlations and injected squeezing, the framework enables high-precision, 3D reconstruction of magnetic field vectors. The approach also demonstrates a resonance condition where cavity-induced noise can be effectively canceled.
main_problem: Conventional steady-state cavity-magnon magnetometry is limited by the loss of initial-state quantum information, inability to detect arbitrary field orientations, and sensitivity to cavity-added noise.
main_result: A transient sensing framework is developed that uses residual initial quantum correlations to enhance SNR and enables 3D magnetic field reconstruction via crosstalk-free quadrature measurements.
method: The authors use Heisenberg-Langevin equations and input-output relations to model transient dynamics, employing a double-difference scheme for vector reconstruction and injected squeezing for noise suppression.
model_or_system: A cavity-magnon system consisting of a microwave cavity coupled to one or more Yttrium Iron Garnet (YIG) spheres, interacting with a 3D magnetic field pulse.
key_observables: Cavity field amplitude and phase quadratures, transient noise spectrum, and signal-to-noise ratio (SNR).
important_parameters: Cavity and magnon decay rates (kappa_a, kappa_m), coupling strength (g_am), squeezing amplitude (r), and the resonance condition g_am = sqrt(kappa_a * kappa_m)/2.
main_assumptions: Assumes a delta-function magnetic field pulse and a specific noise power spectral density for weak magnetic fields.
figures_summary: Fig 2 shows SNR vs frequency and quadrature detection; Fig 5 shows stationary SNR vs frequency and temperature/squeezing thresholds.
paper_structure: The paper introduces the problem of steady-state limitations, describes the transient framework and 3D reconstruction method, analyzes noise suppression via squeezing and resonance, and discusses scalability with multiple YIG spheres.
why_interesting: It provides a novel approach to quantum-limited sensing by exploiting non-equilibrium transient dynamics and engineered initial states to surpass steady-state precision limits.
analysis_basis: full PDF text, analyzed in chunks

figures.caption: FIG. 1. Schematic of the weak magnetic-ﬁeld sensor. A YIG sphere in a microwave cavity couples magnons to cavity photons under an external magnetic ﬁeld Bex(t). A bias ﬁeld Bbˆz tunes the magnon res- onance. The cavity is driven by a microwave source and interfaced with a Josephson parametric ampliﬁer to engineer a squeezed input ﬁeld. The output is measured by homodyne detection, enabling vec- tor magnetic ﬁeld reconstruction.

FIG. 2. (a) Signal-to-noise ratio RNS NR versus normalized frequency ω/κm with the ﬁxed pre-squeezing amplitude r0 = 2. (b) Resonant SNR at ω = 0 as a function of measurement time. (c) RNSNR versus normalized frequency ω/κm with r0 = 2 and κmtm = 3. (d) ⟨m2 p⟩s, (e) ⟨a2 x⟩s, and (f) ⟨axmp + mpax⟩s versus squeezing parameter r0, with ﬁxed squeezing angle θ0 = π. Here r0 and θ0 are the pre-squeezing parameters in the preparation stage, no squeezing is applied during sensing, i.e., (r = θ = 0).

FIG. 3. The detected azimuthal angle (φ) (a) and polar angle (θ) (b) of a weak magnetic ﬁeld using the double-diﬀerence estimator. Solid lines denote true angles, and dashed lines denote estimated values.
FIG. 4. S r(ω) as functions of the frequency ω/κm and the coupling strength gam/κm (a, c) ( of ω/κm and the cavity dissipation rate κa/κm (b, d)) without squeezing (a,b) and with squeezing (c,d).

FIG. 5. (a) Stationary SNR versus normalized frequency ω/κm for diﬀerent squeezing parameters. (b) Stationary SNR as functions of temperature and squeezing parameter, the black solid contour de- notes the threshold RSSNR = 1.
